1: Job continues his response (Job 17 may be part of a single speech begun in Job 16, divided by modern chapter divisions). Rûḥî ḥubbālāh (my spirit is ruined/exhausted) — ḥābal means to bind, pledge, or destroy; here it suggests the spirit has been handed over to destruction. Yamay nizᵃʿākû (my days are extinguished) — the lamp-imagery for life (cf. Job 18:5–6): Job’s days are being put out like a lamp. Qᵉḇārîm lî (the grave for me) — the plural may indicate multiple graves awaiting, or is an intensified form. Death has become Job’s horizon.
2: “Surely there are mockers all around me, and my eye rests on their hostile provocation.” Hᵃtullîm (mockers) surround Job; their mockery is not background noise but foreground torment. Wᵉḇᵉmirāhôṯam tālān ʿênî (my eye rests on their provocation) — the eye that was pouring out tears to God (Job 16:20) now rests on human hostility. This contrast between vertical lament (toward God) and horizontal hostility (from people) structures much of Job’s situation. The same tension appears in Psalm 88 — the most hopeless of the psalms — where the sufferer’s companions become darkness itself.
3: “Give me a pledge yourself; who else is there to strike hands with me?” Śîmāh-nāʾ ʿārᵉḇēnî ʿimmāḵ (place a pledge-surety with yourself for me) — the legal concept of ʿēreḇ (surety, pledge). In ancient Near Eastern commerce and law, a surety was a third party who guaranteed another’s debt or obligation (Genesis 43:9: Judah as surety for Benjamin; Proverbs 6:1–5; Job 11:15; Job 17:18). Job asks God to become his own guarantee — a logical paradox: God pledges to God for Job. The striking-of-hands (mî hûʾ lî yitqāʿ, who will shake hands for me?) was the gesture sealing a surety agreement. Hebrews 7:22 interprets Christ’s priesthood in exactly these terms: “Jesus has become the guarantee of a better covenant.”
4: “For you have closed off their minds from true understanding; therefore you will not let them triumph.” Libbām ṣāpantā mibināh (their heart you have hidden from understanding) — God has concealed understanding from the Friends, which is why they cannot help Job. This is a striking theological claim: the Friends’ inadequacy is not merely intellectual failure but divine action. Their hearts are closed to the understanding that would allow them to serve as genuine advocates. ʿal-kēn lōʾ tᵉrōmēm (therefore you will not let them triumph/be exalted) — the Friends’ inability to vindicate themselves before God is connected to their inability to understand Job’s situation. Divine hardening serves the book’s design: no human wisdom can solve Job’s problem; only God can.
5: “He who betrays his friends for personal gain — his children’s eyes will fail.” This proverbial statement is applied to the Friends: they have betrayed Job (who is their rēaʿ, friend/companion) by choosing comfortable theology over genuine solidarity. Lᵉḥēleq (for a share/portion) — they have a stake in maintaining the retributive system because they benefit from a world where righteousness guarantees prosperity. Their children’s failing eyes (wᵉʿênê bānāyw tiklenāh) is the curse-formula: the sin of the fathers visits the third and fourth generation (Exodus 20:5). The Friends’ theological betrayal of Job is functionally equivalent to betraying a friend for personal gain.
6: “He has turned me into a proverb among the nations — I am one before whose face men spit.” Wᵉhiṣṣîḡanî limšōl ʿammîm (he has set me up as a proverb/byword of the peoples) — being made a māšāl (proverb, taunt-song, byword) is a standard formula of covenant curse in the OT (Deuteronomy 28:37; 1 Kings 9:7; Jeremiah 24:9; Ezekiel 14:8; Psalms 44:15). To become a byword is to become the negative example by which others measure their own fortunate condition. The face-spitting (tōpeṯ-pānîm ʾehyeh) is the extreme humiliation gesture (Numbers 12:14; Deuteronomy 25:9; Isaiah 50:6). Job has descended from honored elder (Job 29:7–25) to social outcast.
7: “My eye has grown dim from sorrow, and all my limbs are like shadows.” Wattikah mikkāʿas ʿênî (my eye has grown dim/exhausted from vexation) — the eye that weeps (Job 16:20), that rests on mockery (Job 17:2), now fails from grief. Yᵉṣārāy kāṣṣēl kullām (my limbs are all like a shadow) — the bodily dissolution that began with flesh-wasting (Job 16:8) reaches the entire body. In the ancient world, the shadow (ṣēl) was associated with both the insubstantial (Psalms 39:6: “man is a mere shadow”; Ecclesiastes 6:12) and with protection (Psalms 17:8; Job 91:1). Job has become the shadow without protection — insubstantial, about to disappear.
8: “Upright men are horrified by this; the innocent rises up against the godless.” A seemingly intrusive verse: the upright men’s horror may be at the injustice of Job’s treatment, or at Job’s apparent descent into impiety. The verse may be Job’s self-location within the community of the righteous: even if the Friends reject him, the yᵉšārîm (upright) and nāqî (innocent) can recognize the injustice. Wᵉṯāqōm ʿal-ḥānēp ḥônēn (the innocent rises against the godless) — Job positioning himself as the innocent who stands against the godless accusers, however the Friends have positioned themselves.
9: “The righteous man keeps to his course, and the one with clean hands grows stronger and stronger.” Despite everything, Job affirms the path of the righteous: wᵉyōsēp ṣaddîq darkô (the righteous man adds to/continues his way) and ûṭᵉhar-yāḏayim yōsîp ʾōmeṣ (the one with clean hands grows stronger). This is an affirmation of moral persistence against circumstantial assault — the righteous do not abandon their course because suffering contradicts their expectation. The verse anticipates the resolution of Job 31, where Job maintains his integrity claim to the end, and stands in tension with the despair of vv1–7 and 11–16 within the same speech.
10: “But come, all of you, try again — I still cannot find a wise man among you.” The challenge to renewed debate: ûlām kullām tāšuḇû-nāʾ (but all of you, please return/try again). Despite the impossibility (“I still cannot find a wise man among you”), Job is not finished. This is the most direct assessment of the Friends’ failure: despite their appeals to tradition, age, and experience, none of them qualifies as a genuine ḥāḵām (wise man) in Job’s assessment. The wisdom tradition’s highest credential — being truly wise — is denied to its self-appointed representatives.
11: “My days are over; my plans have been cut off — even the secret longings of my heart.” Yāmay ʿāḇᵃrû zimōṯay (my days have passed, my purposes/plans) — zimmāh (plan, purpose, thought) here has the positive sense of life-plans and designs, not its common negative sense (wickedness). The cutting off of the heart’s possessions (môrāšê lᵉḇāḇî, the possessions of my heart) — the private longings that never became public — represents the most intimate loss: not just status and family but the unlived hopes that sustained his interior life.
12: “They make night into day; they say, ‘The light is near,’ even while darkness surrounds them.” The Friends’ false comfort: laylāh lᵉyôm yāśîmû (they set night as day) — inverting the order of reality to offer false hope. The optimistic declaration “light is near” (ʾôr qārôḇ mippᵉnê-ḥōšeḵ) in the presence of darkness is the definition of Eliphaz’s consolations (Job 15:11): they claim imminent relief for someone surrounded by darkness. Job refuses this inversion: the darkness he experiences is real; naming it “light” is a lie. This epistemological conflict — reality versus comfortable optimism — is one of the book’s central concerns.
13: “If I wait, Sheol is my home — I have made my bed in the darkness.” Job’s expectation of where hope leads: not to light (v12, the Friends’ false promise) but to Sheol. Im-ʾᵃqawweh šᵉʾôl bêṯî (if I hope/wait, Sheol is my house) — waiting produces only Sheol as dwelling place. Bᵃḥōšeḵ rāpaʿdtî yᵉṣûʿî (in darkness I have spread my bed) — the bed-in-darkness is the most intimate image of dwelling: Job has not just passed through darkness but has made it his permanent resting place. The domestic language (house, bed) applied to Sheol is the extreme form of hopelessness: death has become home.
14: “I call out to corruption, ‘You are my father,’ and to the worm, ‘My mother and my sister.’” The grotesque reversal of covenant-family language: lašṣaḥaṯ qārāʾtî ʾāḇî ʾāttāh (to the pit/corruption I call: you are my father) and lārimmāh ʾimmi wᵉʾᵃḥōṯî (to the worm: my mother and my sister). The intimate family terms (ʾāḇ, father; ʾēm, mother; ʾāḥôṯ, sister) applied to decay and worms is the ultimate estrangement: the human relational network that was Job’s identity (Job 1:1–5) has been replaced by the relational network of dissolution.
15: “Where then is my hope? Who can see hope for me?” Wᵉʾayyēh ʾēpô tiqwāṯî (and where then is my hope?) — tiqwāh (hope) is one of the book’s central terms. In Job 14, Job explored hope under the image of the tree that might sprout (Job 14:7–9) and concluded that, unlike the tree, human death offers no revival. Here the question is even starker: hope itself has no location. The question ûtiqwāṯî mî yᵉšûrennāh (who can see my hope?) suggests hope has become invisible — not just deferred but undetectable. The rhetorical despair here sets the stage for Job 19’s unexpected eruption of hope in the gōʾēl passage.
16: “Will it descend with me to the gates of Sheol? Together we shall go down into the dust.” Baḏḏê šᵉʾôl tēreḏnāh (will they descend to the bars/gates of Sheol?) — the bars (badîm) or gates of Sheol are the portals of the underworld from which no return is possible (Isaiah 38:10: Hezekiah’s lament; Psalms 9:13). Hope descends into the dust alongside Job — hope itself dies. This is the nadir of the speech and among the lowest points of the book before the gōʾēl passage of Job 19:25–27 reverses direction. The structural movement of the book requires this depth: the resolution that comes must be proportionate to the despair expressed.
